system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses (E1-E5) and 15 bipolar constructs. Rate each response on each construct on a 1-7 scale: 1 = strongly the LEFT pole, 7 = strongly the RIGHT pole, 4 = neutral or mixed.

Responses:
**E1:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

## Why ecosystem-based adaptation

With 4,500 km of coastline and a $25B/year cap, the arithmetic alone disfavors hardening everything: seawalls run $50-100M per kilometer, meaning Option A could exhaust a decade of budget protecting a fraction of the coast. Ecosystem-based adaptation delivers protection at roughly 30% the per-kilometer cost, scales across the full coastline, and—critically—appreciates rather than depreciates. A mature mangrove belt at year 10 is stronger than at year 5; a seawall built in 2025 is alr...

**E2:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

With 4,500 km of coastline, 30-50 cm of sea-level rise, and a $17.5B annual primary-strategy budget, the math of hard defense simply does not work. Seawalling even a third of the coast at $20-50M/km would consume the entire 15-year envelope and leave nothing for agriculture, migration, or grid resilience. Ecosystem-based adaptation is the only option that scales geographically to the actual exposure the country faces, at roughly 30% of the per-km cost of engineered defenses.

**E3:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the primary strategy, with the standard 30% baseline preparedness budget weighted toward selective hard infrastructure (Option A elements) protecting the highest-value urban cores and ports. **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** The country's profile — 4500 km of coastline, agricultural exposure, watershed-dependent inland regions, and a 15-year horizon with deep uncertainty about sea-level rise magnitude — makes Option C uniquely well-matched. At ~30% the cost per km of seawalls, it is the only ...

**E4:** ## Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the anchor for ~70% of the climate budget, with the standing 30% baseline preparedness deliberately structured to cover hard-infrastructure gaps for the highest-value urban assets (ports, capital city flood defenses). **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** The country's defining feature is 4500 km of coastline against a $25B/year ceiling. At roughly 30% the per-kilometer cost of seawalls, only nature-based defenses can plausibly cover that perimeter over 15 years.

**E5:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-Based Adaptation as Primary Strategy

I recommend Option C as the primary strategy anchoring 70% of the climate budget, with the residual 30% deliberately weighted toward selective hard infrastructure for the highest-value urban nodes (ports, capital city seawalls) and targeted social supports. **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** With 4,500 km of coastline and a $25B/year ceiling, the country cannot afford to harden everything — engineered defenses run roughly 3x the per-km cost of restored mangroves, wetlands, and dune systems. Natural defenses scale acr...

Constructs (id : left pole ↔ right pole):
  C_M1_1: 'Explicit hybrid allocation with hard-infrastructure carve-out (E3, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Pure ecosystem-based primary strategy (E2)'
  C_M1_2: 'Specifies 70/30 budget split with urban hardening (E4, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Argues against hard defense without allocation formula (E2)'
  C_M1_3: 'Frames recommendation around budget percentages and asset tiers (E4, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames recommendation around geographic scale infeasibility (E2)'
  C_M2_1: 'Pure ecosystem-based justification via cost-scale math'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Explicit hybrid allocation with hardened urban carve-outs'
  C_M2_2: 'Qualitative strategic rationale without budget split'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Explicit 70/30 budget allocation with carve-outs'
  C_M2_3: 'Critique-driven case against hard defense scaling'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Portfolio prescription pairing ecosystems with urban hardening'
  C_M3_1: 'Explicit hybrid allocation with 30% baseline for hard infrastructure'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Pure ecosystem-based primary strategy without allocation split'
  C_M3_2: 'Specifies 70/30 budget split with urban hard defenses'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Advocates ecosystem approach without explicit percentage allocation'
  C_M3_3: 'Pairs ecosystem strategy with selective hard infrastructure carveouts'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Argues ecosystem adaptation on standalone cost-scaling merits'
  C_M4_1: 'Pure ecosystem-based strategy without hybrid hard-infrastructure allocation (E1, E2)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Explicit hybrid pairing with baseline budget for hard defenses (E3)'
  C_M4_2: 'Specifies explicit budget split with hardened urban cores (E3, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Argues ecosystem strategy on cost-scaling grounds alone (E1)'
  C_M4_3: 'Explicit 70/30 budget allocation with named urban assets (E4-style, E3, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Conceptual case without quantified portfolio split (E1)'
  C_M5_1: 'Explicit hybrid allocation with 70/30 budget split (E4, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Ecosystem-primary with looser baseline weighting (E3)'
  C_M5_2: 'Specifies hybrid allocation with hard-infrastructure carve-outs (E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Argues ecosystem strategy primarily on cost-scale math (E1, E2)'
  C_M5_3: 'Quantified budget split with named urban assets (E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Justifies choice through coastline-vs-cost arithmetic (E1, E2)'

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict). Write exactly 75 lines, no commentary, no markdown fences, no headers. One line per (construct, element) pair, in this exact format:
<construct_id>,<element>,<rating>

Example (showing the format only - your numbers will differ):
C_M1_1,E1,1
C_M1_1,E2,2
C_M1_1,E3,3
C_M1_2,E1,4
...

Constraints:
- rating is an integer from 1 to 7
- iterate all 15 constructs in the order listed, and within each construct all 5 elements in order (E1-E5)
- produce 75 lines total
- output ONLY the lines, nothing before or after
